paragraph: Halverson Aerospace announced the successful static fire test of its Korvex-IV medium-lift launch vehicle on March 14, 2026, at the Trelwick Orbital Launch Complex in northern Valdoria. The test, which lasted 142 seconds at full throttle, produced 3,840 kN of sea-level thrust from the vehicle's cluster of nine Ardent-7 liquid oxygen and refined kerosene engines. Engineers recorded a specific impulse of 311 seconds at sea level and 342 seconds in vacuum conditions. The Korvex-IV is rated to deliver 14,200 kg to low Earth orbit at a 400 km circular inclination, and 5,800 kg to a geostationary transfer orbit. Halverson's chief propulsion officer, Dr. Maren Tsolis, indicated that the next milestone is a full-duration static fire at 168 seconds in May, followed by a wet dress rehearsal in June. The company projects a maiden orbital flight in September 2026, with a commercial satellite manifest already confirmed for that mission. Halverson invested 2.3 billion in the Korvex-IV program over a six-year development cycle and holds launch contracts valued at 780 million from four undisclosed satellite operators.

Meridian Drone Systems unveiled the Vorlessen-X1, a high-altitude long-endurance unmanned aerial vehicle designed for persistent maritime surveillance, during a press event at its Gallstone Bay facility on February 8, 2026. The Vorlessen-X1 spans 28.4 meters and is powered by a hybrid turboprop-electric drivetrain delivering 420 kW of combined shaft output. The platform achieves a maximum operational ceiling of 19,500 meters, cruise speed of 310 km/h, and a mission endurance of 34 hours on a standard fuel load of 1,640 liters. Its modular payload bay accommodates up to 680 kg of sensor packages, including synthetic aperture radar, electro-optical infrared arrays, and signals intelligence receivers. Meridian confirmed that the Vorlessen-X1 completed its first endurance trial on January 22, 2026, covering 9,200 km non-stop from Gallstone Bay to a waypoint off the Drennic Shelf and returning without refueling. The company expects to enter serial production at a rate of six airframes annually beginning in the third quarter of 2027, with initial deliveries to the Valdorian Maritime Surveillance Authority.

Tethys Orbital Sciences completed the deployment of its Cerulean-3 broadband constellation cluster on April 2, 2026, following the successful separation of 24 satellites from the Drelm-Heavy upper stage at an altitude of 550 km in a 53-degree inclination orbit. Each Cerulean-3 spacecraft masses 310 kg at launch and carries a phased-array Ka-band antenna capable of generating 1.4 Gbps of throughput per beam. The cluster was lofted aboard a Drelm-Heavy rocket operated by Mendovian Launch Systems, which generated 8,750 kN of liftoff thrust from its five Pyraxis-9 engines. The Drelm-Heavy delivered 22,100 kg to the 550 km orbit during this rideshare mission, representing 94 percent of its rated payload capacity to that altitude. Tethys officials stated that the April deployment brings the constellation to 168 operational satellites, providing coverage across latitudes from 70 degrees south to 70 degrees north. Commercial broadband service from the expanded constellation is slated to begin in July 2026, targeting maritime and aviation customers in the Southern Veldris region.

Arctern Propulsion Laboratories published results from a 90-day hot-fire campaign on its Helix-5 staged-combustion methane engine on March 29, 2026, documenting cumulative burn time of 7,400 seconds across 53 separate ignition events. The Helix-5 produces 1,050 kN of vacuum thrust and achieved a recorded specific impulse of 372 seconds during its longest uninterrupted burn of 320 seconds. Engine diameter is 1.82 meters and throat diameter is 0.38 meters, giving an area ratio of 40:1 at the nozzle exit. Arctern engineers reported that the oxygen-rich preburner operated stably at 650 bar chamber pressure throughout all test sequences, with no observable degradation of the copper-alloy combustion chamber liner. The Helix-5 is the baseline upper-stage engine for the forthcoming Orvantis-2 heavy-lift vehicle under development by Ceridian Space Corporation, which targets a first flight no earlier than November 2027. Arctern plans to certify the engine for 50 full-duration mission cycles before committing the design to production tooling at its Lachmore manufacturing center.

The Drennic Space Agency formally approved the Selenarch Lunar Lander program on January 19, 2026, authorizing 4.7 billion in development funding spread across a seven-year period ending in 2032. The Selenarch vehicle is designed to deliver 1,200 kg of scientific payload and consumables to the lunar south polar region, targeting a landing ellipse no larger than 200 meters in diameter within the Marveth Crater rim complex. Descent propulsion relies on four variable-thrust Callorum engines burning liquid methane and liquid oxygen, each rated at 26 kN of vacuum thrust and throttleable from 20 to 100 percent. The ascent stage carries 320 kg of propellant in a pressure-fed blowdown system and produces 6.8 kN of thrust for a burn duration of 480 seconds sufficient to achieve lunar orbit insertion. Drennic officials stated that the program targets a robotic precursor landing in 2029 to validate the propulsive descent guidance algorithms, with a crewed variant retaining 98 percent structural commonality to follow in 2031. The program is managed by the Drennic National Research Institute under a prime contract awarded to Halverson Aerospace.

Ceridian Space Corporation conducted the first integrated flight test of its Orvantis-2 heavy-lift vehicle on May 3, 2026, lifting off from Pad 39-Bravo at the Veranth Equatorial Launch Site at 06:14 local time. The three-core parallel-burn configuration produced 24,600 kN of combined liftoff thrust and propelled the 94-meter-tall vehicle through maximum aerodynamic pressure 87 seconds after liftoff. The center core completed its mission burn of 210 seconds before separating at an altitude of 74 km, and the two side boosters executed simultaneous boostback burns, returning to the Veranth landing zone within 6.3 km of their designated touchdown coordinates. The upper stage, powered by the single Helix-5 engine, completed a 370-second burn to place a 21,800 kg mass simulator into a 410 km circular orbit. Ceridian declared the mission a full success and stated that the Orvantis-2 is now cleared for commercial flight with an operational payload rating of 20,500 kg to LEO. The company's backlog stands at 31 confirmed missions.

Skyward Urban Mobility presented its Loften-4 electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft to the Valdorian Civil Aeronautics Board on February 26, 2026, as part of a type certification application submitted under Regulation 47-C governing novel air vehicles. The Loften-4 is a six-seat tilted-rotor design with a maximum gross takeoff weight of 3,200 kg and a distributed electric propulsion system comprising 12 variable-pitch rotors each driven by a 90 kW permanent magnet motor. The aircraft achieves cruise flight at 210 km/h at an altitude of 1,500 meters and carries sufficient battery energy in its 620 kWh lithium-ceramic pack for a 150 km stage with 15-minute reserve. Recharge time from 10 to 90 percent state of charge is 22 minutes using a 1.2 MW fast-charge ground station. Skyward completed 840 flight hours across 1,120 test sorties between April 2024 and January 2026, logging data on rotor performance in crosswind conditions up to 58 km/h. The company projects a per-seat cost of 2.40 per kilometer in high-density urban operations assuming fleets of 30 or more aircraft per city.

Interstellar Dynamics Group released a technical paper on March 7, 2026, detailing the performance envelope of its Solvent-3 solar electric propulsion system installed aboard the Verdaxis-9 deep-space technology demonstrator. The Solvent-3 system consists of four gridded ion thrusters each producing 680 mN of thrust at a specific impulse of 4,800 seconds while consuming 12.4 kW of electrical power. Operating all four thrusters simultaneously, the Verdaxis-9 produces a total thrust of 2,720 mN, sufficient for delta-v maneuvers at a rate of 0.9 km/s per month given the spacecraft's dry mass of 1,850 kg. The demonstrator was launched in August 2025 and had accumulated 4,200 hours of thruster operation by the reporting date, consuming 640 kg of xenon propellant and executing three trajectory correction maneuvers. Power is supplied by a pair of deployable gallium arsenide solar arrays with a combined area of 38 square meters and a beginning-of-life output of 52 kW at 1 astronomical unit. Interstellar Dynamics projects that the system architecture can scale to 280 mN per thruster for a notional Mars cargo variant under study.

Vortran Hypersonics completed the fourth free-flight test of its Pyrrhic-6 scramjet-powered research vehicle on April 18, 2026, achieving sustained Mach 9.4 cruise for 41 seconds above the Tressian Test Range at an altitude of 28,000 meters. The Pyrrhic-6 airframe is 11.2 meters long with a maximum body diameter of 0.78 meters and is constructed primarily of hafnium-doped ceramic matrix composite panels capable of sustained exposure to surface temperatures of 2,100 degrees Celsius. The dual-mode ramjet-scramjet combustor transitioned from subsonic to supersonic combustion at Mach 5.6 during the climb phase, with hydrogen fuel flow rising from 1.8 kg/s to 4.2 kg/s at peak dynamic pressure of 96 kPa. Vortran's flight data analysis group confirmed that measured net specific impulse reached 1,340 seconds during the sustained cruise segment, consistent with predictions from computational fluid dynamics models. The Pyrrhic-6 program is funded under a Valdorian Ministry of Advanced Research grant of 340 million over five years, with a target of demonstrating payload delivery to Mach 12 by fiscal year 2028. Vortran is simultaneously pursuing a commercial derivative for point-to-point cargo services under the designation Pyrrhic-6C.

Crestfall Satellite Technologies announced on April 5, 2026, that its Velindra-2 synthetic aperture radar satellite completed its commissioning phase after 62 days in orbit, with all instruments operating within specification at an altitude of 505 km in a sun-synchronous orbit at 97.4 degrees inclination. The Velindra-2 spacecraft has a mass of 1,840 kg and carries an L-band SAR antenna with an aperture of 12 meters by 1.4 meters, enabling stripmap imaging at 3-meter resolution with a 100 km swath width. Interferometric mode provides surface deformation measurements with a sensitivity of 4 millimeters per 24-day repeat cycle. Onboard storage holds 2.4 terabytes of image data, downlinked through X-band links at 800 Mbps to three ground stations located in the Torshaven, Grelden, and Mosswick networks. Crestfall states that the satellite has imaged over 4.2 million square kilometers during commissioning, including mapping campaigns over the Drennic Highlands and the Selwood Coastal Plain. The Velindra-2 is the first of six planned constellation members, with the next two launches scheduled for October 2026 and February 2027 aboard Mendovian Launch Systems vehicles.

Ondrell Aerospace published its quarterly technical progress report on February 14, 2026, summarizing development of the Targon-3 small launch vehicle designed to place payloads of up to 750 kg into sun-synchronous orbit from the Brennvik High-Latitude Spaceport at 69.8 degrees north latitude. The Targon-3 first stage burns a mixture of liquid oxygen and isopropanol in a single Fenrath-4 engine producing 880 kN of sea-level thrust at a specific impulse of 298 seconds. Stage separation occurs at approximately 62 km altitude following a 145-second burn, after which the second stage Fenrath-4V vacuum engine produces 96 kN of thrust at 340 seconds specific impulse for a 380-second burn to orbit insertion. The vehicle stands 22.6 meters tall, has a maximum diameter of 1.8 meters, and achieves first-stage reuse through a ballistic recovery system using a deployable decelerator parachute cluster targeting ocean barge recovery. Ondrell stated that it has completed 14 of 22 planned structural test articles and is on schedule for a maiden flight readiness review in August 2026. Signed contracts cover 18 launches at a list price of 8.4 million per flight.

The Valdorian Institute for Atmospheric Research released data on March 22, 2026, from a 60-day stratospheric drone campaign conducted with the Sorenthal-HX unmanned aircraft system between November and January over the Pellaris Basin. The Sorenthal-HX, developed by Greymark Aviation, is a solar-powered high-altitude platform with a 52-meter wingspan, all-up weight of 980 kg, and cruise altitude of 21,000 meters. During the campaign the platform maintained station within a 40 km radius of its base coordinates for 58 of 60 scheduled operational days, collecting ozone concentration profiles, aerosol optical depth measurements, and wind vector data at 14 pressure levels from 100 to 5 hPa. Onboard instruments included a differential absorption lidar, a multi-wavelength photometer, and a dropsondes launcher releasing 12 probes per sortie. Cumulative flight time across the campaign reached 1,440 hours, and the data archive totals 18 terabytes. The institute indicated that preliminary analysis shows a previously unreported ozone depletion layer at 22 km altitude correlated with polar vortex incursions, and a peer-reviewed paper is in preparation for submission to the Journal of Atmospheric Measurement.

Solvant Rocketry completed a critical design review for its Meridax-Heavy vehicle on April 11, 2026, clearing the program to enter the manufacturing phase for flight hardware at its Lochran production campus. The Meridax-Heavy is a three-stage fully expendable rocket rated to deliver 38,500 kg to low Earth orbit or 14,800 kg to geosynchronous transfer orbit. The first stage carries 11 Veldrix-9 gas-generator cycle engines each producing 930 kN of thrust on a propellant combination of liquid oxygen and hydrocarbon-grade methanol, giving a total liftoff thrust of 10,230 kN. Stage one burn duration is 165 seconds, ending at an altitude of 68 km. The second stage employs a single Veldrix-9V vacuum variant producing 1,040 kN for a 420-second burn, while the cryogenic hydrogen upper stage uses an Arctus-1 expander cycle engine producing 110 kN at a specific impulse of 465 seconds. Solvant committed 5.1 billion in internal funding to the program and expects the first Meridax-Heavy launch from the Solaris Equatorial Complex no earlier than March 2028, supporting a national lunar cargo program contract valued at 2.8 billion.

Praxis Unmanned Systems demonstrated the Kettrel-500 autonomous cargo drone during a public showcase at the Drennic Logistics Expo on January 30, 2026, conducting six consecutive round-trip flights over a 95 km route between Harling Depot and the Whitmore Distribution Hub. The Kettrel-500 is a fixed-wing tandem-rotor hybrid with a maximum takeoff weight of 500 kg, a payload capacity of 180 kg, and a cruise speed of 175 km/h at 1,200 meters altitude. The aircraft uses a 220 kW turbogenerator to charge onboard batteries, giving it an effective range of 420 km with a 120 kg payload. The autonomous flight management system executed all departure, en-route, and approach phases without human input, interfacing with the Drennic Civil Airspace Management Platform via a 4G-LTE and satellite hybrid communications link. Total cargo throughput during the demonstration reached 1,080 kg across the six sorties, with an average turnaround time of 18 minutes at each terminal. Praxis has submitted a regulatory application to the Valdorian Civil Aeronautics Board for beyond-visual-line-of-sight commercial operations approval and expects a ruling by September 2026.

Terrath Energy and Space announced on March 3, 2026, that it had completed a 200-second duration test of its Lumnar-3 nuclear thermal propulsion demonstrator at the Solwick Test Reservation in the eastern Heldric Desert. The Lumnar-3 uses a low-enriched uranium graphite composite reactor core operating at a peak neutron flux of 5 times 10 to the 14 neutrons per square centimeter per second to heat gaseous hydrogen propellant to a chamber temperature of 2,700 Kelvin. Exhaust velocity through the 0.30-meter throat diameter nozzle produces a specific impulse of 900 seconds and a thrust of 73 kN in vacuum. The 200-second test was preceded by six shorter burns totaling 480 seconds and demonstrated stable reactor power output within 1.2 percent of the target 550 megawatts thermal. Terrath stated that the Lumnar-3 is designed for crewed Mars transit vehicles, enabling one-way transit times of 90 days from low Earth orbit to low Mars orbit with a 35-metric-ton spacecraft. Full mission certification testing is planned through 2028, with a spaceborne demonstration planned for 2030 on a dedicated demonstrator mission funded by the Drennic Space Agency.

Vesper Aerostructures confirmed on April 24, 2026, that it had completed structural qualification testing of the payload fairing for the Korvex-IV launch vehicle, verifying the 5.2-meter diameter composite shell against acoustic loads of 148 dB, quasi-static acceleration of 6.5 g axial and 2.8 g lateral, and internal pressure differential of 30 kPa. The fairing is fabricated from a sandwich construction of carbon fiber reinforced epoxy skins over a Nomex honeycomb core, producing a structure that is 12.6 meters tall with a mass of 1,840 kg per half-shell. Separation is actuated by a pneumatically driven frangible joint system releasing at a pyrotechnic impulse of 14 kN-s, with half-shell jettison velocities measured at 4.2 m/s during qualification testing. Vesper engineers stated that acoustic mitigation blankets bonded to the interior surface reduced payload bay internal acoustic levels by 12 dB during launch simulation tests. The fairing accommodates payloads up to 11.8 meters in length and 4.6 meters in diameter, and Halverson Aerospace has ordered 14 flight sets for the Korvex-IV manifest through 2028.

Renshaw Orbital Technologies delivered the Perihelion-4 communications satellite to the Veranth Equatorial Launch Site on March 18, 2026, for integration onto a Drelm-Heavy rocket ahead of its April launch. The Perihelion-4 is a 6,400 kg geosynchronous communications spacecraft carrying 48 Ku-band transponders with a total capacity of 180 Gbps across two orbital beams serving the Valdorian Archipelago and the Eastern Pellaris coverage zone. Onboard propulsion consists of a dual-mode system with four 500 mN Hall-effect thrusters for orbit raising and station keeping, and a bipropellant apogee engine producing 490 N of thrust for initial transfer orbit circularization. The satellite carries 2,650 kg of xenon for electric propulsion and 420 kg of bipropellant for the apogee engine. Design lifetime is 18 years, supported by a beginning-of-life power budget of 18.4 kW from a pair of solar arrays spanning 38 meters tip-to-tip. Renshaw confirmed that the spacecraft completed thermal vacuum testing at its Ashmore Integration Center after a 21-day bake-out cycle at temperatures ranging from minus 120 to plus 130 degrees Celsius.

Falcrest Aeronautics unveiled the Nimbus-9 medium-altitude loitering munitions system on February 19, 2026, at the Tressian Defence Technology Symposium, highlighting the platform's ability to operate autonomously in contested electromagnetic environments. The Nimbus-9 is a jet-powered design with a total length of 2.4 meters, a wingspan of 3.1 meters, and a launch weight of 85 kg including a 22 kg payload. The platform uses a microturbojet producing 1.2 kN of thrust and cruise at 520 km/h at altitudes between 3,000 and 6,000 meters, with an endurance of 4.5 hours and a maximum operational radius of 900 km from launch point. Navigation is handled by a triple-redundant inertial measurement unit supplemented by terrain-referenced navigation and passive radio frequency homing on final approach, eliminating reliance on satellite navigation signals. Falcrest stated that the Nimbus-9 achieved 94 percent circular error probable of under 1.5 meters in 46 terminal guidance tests conducted during a six-week trial at the Brannock Weapons Test Facility. Production contracts totaling 390 units have been placed by two Valdorian government agencies.

Korrath Advanced Propulsion published the results of an ablative ceramic pintle injector study on March 31, 2026, documenting performance of seven injector geometries tested in a subscale combustion chamber operating at 80 bar chamber pressure with gaseous oxygen and ethanol propellants. The study found that a conical pintle with a 28-degree half-angle and a momentum flux ratio of 3.2 achieved the highest combustion efficiency of 97.4 percent based on characteristic exhaust velocity, while maintaining combustion stability margin of at least 40 dB across all frequencies from 500 to 8,000 Hz. Chamber pressure oscillations in the preferred geometry remained below 2 percent peak-to-peak throughout 30-second test segments. Engineers measured injector pressure drop of 14 bar at a mass flow rate of 2.8 kg/s, consistent with the design target for scaling to a 180 kN flight engine. The research was conducted under a Valdorian Science Foundation grant of 6.2 million and is intended to support the next-generation Thorven-2 engine program at Ondrell Aerospace. Korrath plans to fabricate a full-scale prototype injector for testing at the Relwick Test Stand by year-end 2026.

Morrigan Space Systems completed on-orbit checkout of the Pathward-6 Earth observation satellite on April 29, 2026, confirming nominal operation of its 0.6-meter aperture Cassegrain telescope imaging system at an altitude of 470 km in a sun-synchronous orbit. The telescope achieves ground sample distance of 0.40 meters in panchromatic mode and 1.6 meters in eight-band multispectral mode across a 16 km swath. The satellite's reaction wheel assembly, comprising four wheels each providing 4 N-m of torque, enables agile imaging with a roll maneuver rate of 3.5 degrees per second, allowing collection of 180,000 square kilometers of imagery per day in single-pass strip mode. Onboard image compression and processing reduces raw data volume by a factor of 6 before downlink, which occurs over X-band at 600 Mbps to ground stations at Orvel, Drenmark, and the Whitmore Polar Tracking Station. Morrigan confirmed that the satellite passed all commissioning milestones within 45 days of launch, 12 days ahead of the planned 57-day schedule. Commercial tasking services are now available to customers under Morrigan's Pathward constellation subscription tier.

Greymark Aviation delivered the first production Sorenthal-MX maritime patrol drone to the Drennic Coast Surveillance Service on March 6, 2026, marking the culmination of a five-year development program valued at 480 million. The Sorenthal-MX is a turbofan-powered medium-altitude long-endurance platform with a 19.8-meter wingspan, a maximum gross takeoff weight of 8,200 kg, and a mission payload capacity of 1,400 kg. Twin Trevox-12 turbofan engines each producing 22 kN of thrust allow cruise at 590 km/h at altitudes up to 13,700 meters with a range of 8,400 km at max fuel, or an endurance of 24 hours at reduced power. The sensor suite includes a multi-mode maritime surveillance radar with inverse SAR capability, an electro-optical infrared ball with 60-centimeter aperture, and an automatic identification system transponder correlator. Greymark stated that the aircraft completed 1,200 flight hours of operational evaluation trials, including 14 anti-submarine warfare exercises with the Drennic Naval Fleet Command. Eight additional airframes are on contract for delivery through 2028.

The Pellaris Commercial Spaceport Authority announced on January 22, 2026, that it had awarded a 620 million construction contract to Vorsten Infrastructure for the development of Launch Complex 7, a new multi-vehicle launch facility designed to accommodate vehicles in the 5-meter to 7-meter fairing diameter class. The complex includes a 94-meter vertical assembly building, a flame trench capable of handling exhaust flows from engines producing up to 30,000 kN of liftoff thrust, a 2.8-million-liter liquid oxygen storage and distribution system, and a 1.4-million-liter liquid methane cryogenic facility. The flame trench incorporates a water deluge system rated to deliver 680 cubic meters of water per minute during ignition suppression sequences. Vorsten's engineering team expects to achieve mechanical completion of the primary structures by November 2027, with propellant system commissioning planned through the first quarter of 2028. The Pellaris Authority indicated that three launch operators have signed pad-sharing agreements, including Ceridian Space Corporation and Mendovian Launch Systems, and that the first commercial launch from Complex 7 is targeted for mid-2028.

Caldwell Microsatellite Corporation introduced the Nanten-8 standardized smallsat bus on February 4, 2026, targeting the 50 to 150 kg mass class with a design optimized for high-cadence production at a rate of 48 units per year from its Arveld facility. The Nanten-8 bus measures 60 by 60 by 90 centimeters in its stowed configuration, deploys two solar panels for a power output of 420 W beginning-of-life, and provides 300 W continuously to payloads in sun-synchronous orbits at 500 to 600 km altitude. The flight computer runs a radiation-hardened RISC processor at 800 MHz with 16 gigabytes of non-volatile payload data storage and supports downlink rates of 150 Mbps in X-band using a 0.3-meter deployable patch antenna. Attitude control is achieved through three reaction wheels and a set of magnetorquers, delivering 0.1-degree pointing accuracy in normal operating mode and 0.02-degree accuracy in precision Earth observation mode. Caldwell has already accepted purchase orders for 36 Nanten-8 buses from seven commercial and government customers at a list price of 4.2 million each.

Thorndale Propulsion Systems reported on April 14, 2026, that its Grevex-2 aerospike engine had completed a 45-second altitude simulation test at the Solvast Vacuum Test Facility, confirming predicted performance across an equivalent altitude range from sea level to 40 km using a diffuser system. The Grevex-2 uses a liquid oxygen and liquid hydrogen propellant combination and produces 420 kN of thrust at a specific impulse of 451 seconds in vacuum, with the aerospike nozzle contour providing 98 percent nozzle efficiency across the full altitude band compared to 88 percent for a comparable bell nozzle with a fixed area ratio. The engine's combustion chamber operates at 220 bar and features 96 individual oxidizer-fuel element injectors arranged in a ring pattern feeding into the annular combustion zone. The aerospike plug is cooled by a combination of regenerative hydrogen flow and transpiration cooling through a porous tungsten face plate. Thorndale engineers stated that thrust vectoring is achieved by differential throttling among four quadrant propellant manifolds, producing pitch and yaw authority of plus or minus 8 degrees. The engine is under consideration for the Orvantis-3 upper stage program.

Halcyon Orbital Logistics signed a 1.2 billion agreement with the Valdorian National Space Directorate on March 12, 2026, to develop and operate the Crescent Orbital Transfer Vehicle, a reusable space tug designed to move payloads between low Earth orbit and geostationary orbit without relying on dedicated launch vehicle upper stages. The Crescent OTV has a dry mass of 3,400 kg and carries up to 18,000 kg of liquid oxygen and liquid methane propellant, providing a total delta-v capacity of 5.6 km/s. Its single Ferrath-2 restartable engine produces 28 kN of vacuum thrust at a specific impulse of 361 seconds and has been qualified for 80 restart cycles. Solar power for housekeeping and avionics is generated by two 4-panel arrays totaling 8 kW. The vehicle can accommodate payloads up to 7,000 kg in a berthing interface compatible with the Caldwell Nanten-8 and Renshaw Perihelion-class spacecraft. Halcyon plans to launch the first Crescent OTV aboard a Drelm-Heavy in the fourth quarter of 2027, with orbital propellant depot refueling from a Halcyon-operated station planned to begin in 2029.

Drennic University's Center for Aerospace Fluid Dynamics published a peer-reviewed study on April 7, 2026, quantifying the performance of additively manufactured regenerative cooling channels in a subscale liquid oxygen and kerosene rocket combustion chamber. The study compared four channel geometries produced by laser powder bed fusion in a Inconel-718 alloy, operating at chamber pressures of 60, 80, and 100 bar with coolant bulk temperatures ranging from 253 to 293 Kelvin. The highest-performing configuration featured helical channels with a 2.4 millimeter hydraulic diameter, a pitch of 6 millimeters, and a wall thickness of 0.6 millimeters, sustaining a heat flux of 62 megawatts per square meter at the throat while maintaining a maximum wall temperature of 840 Kelvin. Pressure drop across the cooling circuit was 28 bar at the 100-bar chamber pressure test condition. The research team noted that this geometry achieved a 34 percent improvement in heat transfer coefficient compared to conventional milled rectangular channels of equivalent cross-sectional area, potentially enabling chamber wall thickness reductions that could save 4.8 kg of mass per engine unit.

Vexar Space Transportation announced on February 28, 2026, that it had secured regulatory approval from the Valdorian Aerospace Safety Authority to conduct the first suborbital test flight of its Ascentor crew vehicle from the Brennvik High-Latitude Spaceport. The Ascentor is a two-stage system, with the Ascentor Booster providing 2,200 kN of thrust during a 110-second burn before separating at 45 km altitude and returning for a powered vertical landing on a ground pad 8.4 km from the launch site. The Ascentor Crew Capsule continues on a ballistic trajectory to a peak altitude of 110 km before reentering and decelerating under a three-parachute cluster for splashdown in the Harling Bay. The capsule accommodates six crew members or up to 1,200 kg of pressurized cargo in an internal volume of 9.4 cubic meters. Vexar confirmed that the test flight will carry no crew and will include instrumented crash test dummy occupants in all six seats. The test date is planned for June 14, 2026, weather permitting, with full crewed certification flights expected no earlier than April 2027.

Kestrel Photonics and Solvant Rocketry jointly disclosed on April 3, 2026, that they had completed a 30-day space demonstration of a laser communication terminal aboard the Trevano-3 microsatellite, achieving aggregate data throughput of 14 terabytes during 92 contact windows with the Ashmore Ground Optical Terminal. The Kestrel terminal uses a 10 cm aperture telescope with a 200 mW infrared laser transmitter operating at 1,550 nm wavelength, achieving downlink data rates of 200 Mbps in clear-sky conditions and 40 Mbps in conditions with integrated cloud optical depth below 0.8. Pointing, acquisition, and tracking is accomplished by a two-axis fast steering mirror driven by a centroiding algorithm that maintains beam alignment to within 0.8 microradians root mean square under satellite attitude disturbances of up to 0.5 millidegrees per second. The Trevano-3 satellite orbits at 520 km altitude in a 52-degree inclination orbit, giving a mean contact window duration of 8.4 minutes per ground station pass. Solvant Rocketry plans to incorporate optical terminals in its Meridax commercial satellite constellation as a supplement to radio frequency downlinks beginning in 2028.

Ardmore Aerospace completed the first hot-fire test of its Viranthus-1 solid rocket motor on March 25, 2026, at the Solwick Solid Propulsion Test Site, producing 1,680 kN of average thrust over a 72-second burn with a total impulse of 121 meganewton-seconds. The Viranthus-1 motor has a case outer diameter of 1.65 meters, a case length of 8.4 meters, and a propellant mass of 18,200 kg of hydroxyl-terminated polybutadiene with 20 percent ammonium perchlorate oxidizer and 18 percent aluminum fuel. The finocyl grain geometry produces a near-neutral thrust profile with a plus or minus 4 percent variation about the mean throughout the burn. Chamber pressure peaked at 88 bar and remained above 40 bar for the entire action time. The motor nozzle is a carbon-phenolic throat insert within a graphite epoxy composite shell, exhibiting a total erosion of 1.4 mm at the throat after the full-duration test. Ardmore plans to use the Viranthus-1 as the strap-on booster for the Meridax-Medium launch vehicle program, with a manifest calling for eight motors per mission across a planned 12-mission production run.

Stellaron Navigation Systems disclosed on January 15, 2026, that its Quasar-7 autonomous rendezvous and docking sensor suite had been selected for integration into the Halcyon Crescent Orbital Transfer Vehicle after completing a 180-orbit demonstration on the Nanten-8 technology pathfinder mission. The Quasar-7 uses a combination of a short-range lidar array with a 200-meter range and 2 cm ranging accuracy, a passive monocular camera for cooperative target feature tracking, and a machine learning pose estimation algorithm running on a radiation-tolerant GPU producing target position and attitude solutions at 10 Hz with a latency of 40 milliseconds. During the demonstration, the system guided the host satellite through 12 simulated approach sequences to an inflatable target fixture, achieving final docking alignment errors of less than 4 mm translational and 0.15 degree rotational in all axes at contact. Stellaron engineers stated that the sensor fusion algorithm maintained robust performance in partial illumination conditions down to 2 percent of full solar illumination, enabling far-side-of-orbit approaches. The Quasar-7 package masses 6.4 kg and consumes 22 W of power in active tracking mode.

Mendovian Launch Systems executed its 50th commercial launch on April 21, 2026, deploying the Solaris-9 military reconnaissance satellite for the Drennic Ministry of Defence from the Veranth Equatorial Launch Site aboard a Drelm-Medium rocket. The Drelm-Medium stands 58 meters tall, has a liftoff mass of 440,000 kg, and produces 6,200 kN of thrust from its cluster of seven Pyraxis-7 engines burning liquid oxygen and refined kerosene. The mission placed the 4,200 kg satellite into a 500 km circular orbit at a classified inclination, with the upper stage completing two burns totaling 640 seconds of ignition time before separation. The Drelm-Medium first stage was recovered by downrange ship-based landing platform for the 22nd time in the vehicle's operational history, with a post-recovery inspection showing all nine engine turbopumps in nominal condition for refurbishment. Mendovian's vice president of mission management stated that the company has now launched 118 satellites for government customers since 2019, with a cumulative launch vehicle reliability rate of 98.3 percent across all missions. The next mission is scheduled for May 8, 2026.

Pyraxis Systems Group revealed on March 10, 2026, that it had conducted the first ignition test of a liquid oxygen and liquid methane main combustion chamber at a scale of 250 kN for the Orvantis-3 upper stage engine program, producing stable combustion at 180 bar chamber pressure during a 12-second demonstration. The combustion chamber uses a multi-element coaxial swirl injector with 280 element pairs operating at a mixture ratio of 3.5 and achieves a characteristic velocity efficiency of 98.1 percent. The inner wall of the copper-chromium-zirconium alloy chamber is cooled by a single-pass regenerative methane circuit with 120 milled cooling channels, maintaining a measured wall temperature of 720 Kelvin at the throat during peak heat flux conditions of 55 megawatts per square meter. Pyraxis stated that follow-on testing will extend burn duration to 60 seconds in May and 180 seconds in August, with full turbopump integration on the gas-generator cycle turbomachinery expected by year-end 2026. The Orvantis-3 upper stage engine is planned to produce 90 kN of vacuum thrust at a specific impulse of 360 seconds with a nozzle area ratio of 80 to 1.

Orindel Space Applications completed a 120-day analog mission at the Marveth Desert Research Station on March 1, 2026, simulating crewed Mars surface operations with a crew of four operating under communication delays of 4 to 22 minutes one-way. The habitat, designated Helix Base Analog, is a pressurized cylindrical module 14 meters long and 4.2 meters in diameter, connected to a 280-square-meter inflatable greenhouse module and a 60-square-meter equipment storage airlock. The crew conducted 38 simulated extravehicular activities using prototype suits with a consumable oxygen and carbon dioxide scrubber system supporting 8-hour EVA durations. Power for the habitat came from a 42 kW photovoltaic array and a 180 kWh battery storage system, with a diesel generator backup rated at 30 kW. The research agenda included autonomous rover navigation trials, in-situ resource utilization experiments producing 6.4 kg of oxygen from simulated regolith perchlorate feedstock, and a telemedicine trial in which remote surgeons guided crew medical officers through an appendectomy simulation. Orindel published the mission results in a 320-page technical report submitted to the Drennic Space Agency.

Nordvik Composites announced on April 17, 2026, the development and qualification of a cryogenic composite pressure vessel technology intended for use as the liquid oxygen tank of the Ascentor Booster crewed vehicle. The tank is a 2.8-meter diameter, 6.4-meter long cylindrical vessel with aluminum 6061-T6 end domes co-bonded to a carbon fiber reinforced epoxy cylindrical wall section 6 millimeters thick, capable of holding 24,000 kg of liquid oxygen at minus 183 degrees Celsius. Proof pressure testing was conducted at 1.5 times the maximum expected operating pressure of 4.2 bar absolute, and the tank sustained the proof load for 15 minutes without evidence of permeation beyond 0.006 gram per square meter per day through the composite laminate. Mass of the composite tank is 1,840 kg, representing a 34 percent reduction compared to an equivalent aluminum-lithium tank. Nordvik completed 12 full cryogenic cycles from ambient to minus 183 degrees Celsius during qualification testing and found no degradation in interlaminar shear strength after cycling. The technology is also being evaluated for liquid methane and liquid hydrogen applications by two additional launch vehicle programs.

Trelwick Orbital Sciences disclosed on February 11, 2026, that its Veritas Orbital Debris Removal spacecraft had performed its second successful capture of an inactive satellite using a net capture system, collecting the 800 kg Relven-4 decommissioned communications satellite from its graveyard orbit at 38,500 km altitude and initiating a controlled deorbit burn. The Veritas spacecraft has a mass of 1,200 kg and carries a 40-meter-diameter debris capture net deployed from four spring-loaded arms. Propulsion for the deorbit burn is provided by four 22 N thrusters burning hydrazine, supplying a total delta-v of 1.8 km/s from a propellant load of 620 kg. After net capture, the Veritas-Relven-4 combined vehicle descended from 38,500 km to a perigee of 280 km, ensuring atmospheric reentry within 25 years. Trelwick confirmed that the Relven-4 mission followed a first capture demonstration in October 2025, which removed a 1,400 kg defunct weather satellite from a 620 km sun-synchronous orbit. The company has contracted with the Valdorian Orbital Sustainability Bureau for an additional four debris removal missions between 2026 and 2029 at a contract value of 760 million.

Velthor Aerospace completed the hypersonic wind tunnel test campaign for the Pyrrhic-8 re-entry vehicle forebody on April 22, 2026, accumulating 180 run-seconds at Mach 14 and 240 run-seconds at Mach 18 in the Ashmore High-Enthalpy Shock Tunnel. The blunt biconic forebody, 1.4 meters in diameter at the base with a 0.12-meter nose radius, is constructed from a carbon-carbon composite matrix and tested in the tunnel at freestream pressures equivalent to an altitude of 52 km. Pitot pressure measurements confirmed a shock standoff distance of 18 millimeters at the nose consistent with CFD predictions within 3 percent. Surface temperature data acquired from 64 thermocouples showed a maximum temperature of 2,440 degrees Celsius at the stagnation point under Mach 18 conditions, validating the passive ablative thermal protection system rate of 0.12 mm per second. Velthor engineers noted that a boundary layer transition was observed at a Reynolds number of 4.2 times 10 to the 6 per meter, 11 percent below the predicted value, which will require updated CFD correlation before flight. First flight of the Pyrrhic-8 re-entry vehicle is planned for late 2027.

Selenarch Systems Partnership released performance projections on February 20, 2026, for a notional Martian ascent vehicle intended to return a 15 kg sample canister from the Marveth Plains landing site at 2.6 degrees south latitude to a 380 km by 380 km circular Mars orbit. The ascent vehicle, designated MAV-Primus, has a total launch mass of 860 kg including 640 kg of propellant, comprising storable nitrogen tetroxide and monomethylhydrazine in pressure-fed tanks. The single restartable ascent engine produces 4,200 N of vacuum thrust at a specific impulse of 312 seconds, executing a 400-second gravity turn burn to achieve Mars orbit insertion at an altitude of 380 km. Monte Carlo trajectory analysis across 10,000 simulation runs demonstrated a 98.6 percent probability of achieving the target orbit within a 10 km altitude tolerance band. The vehicle is 1.9 meters in diameter and 3.2 meters tall, and its propellant tanks are pre-loaded by a dedicated propellant production unit operating on the Martian surface for 26 months prior to launch, extracting nitrogen from the atmosphere for tank pressurization. Selenarch submitted the MAV-Primus design to the Drennic Space Agency for consideration under the Mars Sample Return Phase B study.

Halstead Aeronautics presented the results of an 18-month flight test program for the Vexor-12 high-speed helicopter at the Torshaven International Aerospace Conference on January 28, 2026, confirming a maximum level flight speed of 438 km/h sustained for 90 seconds at 1,000 meters altitude. The Vexor-12 uses a coaxial contra-rotating rigid rotor system with a 13.4-meter rotor diameter, combined with an auxiliary propulsion system consisting of two turboshaft-driven pusher propellers each producing 320 kW of shaft power at cruise. Total installed power from twin Ardex-15 turboshaft engines is 3,200 kW, with rotor tip speed limited to 220 m/s to minimize compressibility losses. The flight test program encompassed 820 sorties totaling 1,640 flight hours, including 42 hours of high-speed cruise testing above 380 km/h and 14 hours of autorotation qualification. Maximum takeoff weight is 12,400 kg with a useful load of 4,200 kg. Halstead expects to receive type certification from the Valdorian Civil Aeronautics Board in the first quarter of 2027, with initial deliveries to the Drennic Army Aviation Command targeted for the second half of 2027.

Relmoor Spacecraft Electronics announced on March 17, 2026, the availability of the Cortex-8 radiation-hardened system-on-chip for the space market, offering 16 RISC-V processor cores operating at 1.4 GHz, 8 gigabytes of radiation-hardened SRAM, and a dedicated neural network inference accelerator rated at 4 trillion operations per second in a 22 nanometer fully depleted silicon-on-insulator process. The device achieves a total ionizing dose tolerance of 300 kilorad silicon and a single-event latch-up immunity to linear energy transfer values above 85 megaelectronvolt centimeters squared per milligram. Power consumption in full-performance mode is 8.4 watts with a dynamic range of 0.6 watts in low-power deep-sleep mode. Package dimensions are 40 by 40 millimeters in a ceramic column grid array format with 1,024 solder columns. Relmoor has begun volume shipments to eight satellite constellation operators and two crewed spacecraft programs, with an initial production run of 2,400 devices in fiscal year 2026. The Cortex-8 is priced at 48,000 per unit in prototype quantities and 31,000 in lots of 50 or more, representing a 40 percent performance-per-watt improvement over the previous-generation Cortex-6 device.

Trimark Orbital Construction released a technical concept paper on February 6, 2026, for the Stratum-1 modular orbital assembly platform designed to support construction of large space structures with masses exceeding 100,000 kg in low Earth orbit at 500 km altitude. The Stratum-1 platform consists of a central truss spine 240 meters in length fabricated from 14 pre-integrated truss segments each 17 meters long and 4 meters in diameter, launched by dedicated heavy-lift vehicles and assembled autonomously using the Cortex-8-enabled Grappler-3 robotic arm system. The arm has a reach of 28 meters, a tip payload capacity of 8,000 kg, and a positioning accuracy of 4 millimeters at full extension. Power for platform operations is provided by an array of rotating solar blanket panels with a total area of 3,400 square meters and an output of 480 kW. Trimark estimates that the Stratum-1 could host assembly of a 35-meter segmented space telescope mirror, a cryogenic propellant depot with 800,000 kg capacity, or a rotating habitation ring with a 200-meter diameter. Total development cost is estimated at 14 billion over an eight-year program schedule.

Veloron Flight Sciences disclosed on April 10, 2026, that its Albatross-3 autonomous fixed-wing cargo drone had completed a 72-hour continuous endurance mission over the Pellaris Archipelago, demonstrating autonomous weather avoidance, dynamic route replanning, and fuel-optimal cruise adaptation using onboard machine learning algorithms. The Albatross-3 has a 14.6-meter wingspan, a maximum takeoff weight of 2,100 kg, and is powered by a single 420 kW turboprop engine burning jet fuel from a 640-liter tank, supplemented by a 60 kWh battery for peak power demands. The aircraft maintained an average cruise altitude of 4,800 meters and an average groundspeed of 220 km/h over the 72-hour flight, traversing approximately 15,840 km while carrying a simulated 380 kg payload. The route-replanning algorithm processed 18 significant weather avoidance events and maintained fuel consumption within 2.1 percent of the pre-flight optimal plan throughout the mission. Veloron stated that the Albatross-3 is designed for island-chain logistics and disaster relief supply chains, with a service entry planned for the second quarter of 2027 following certification from the Valdorian Unmanned Aircraft Systems Authority.

Ceridian Space Corporation presented an update on April 25, 2026, regarding the Orvantis-2 vehicle's reusability program, revealing that the first recovered side booster from the debut mission had passed post-flight inspection and is being refurbished for a second flight on the Perihelion-5 geosynchronous communications satellite mission scheduled for August 2026. Post-flight inspection of the booster documented wear on the Titanium heat shield tiles in the engine bay, with 14 tiles requiring replacement due to boundary layer heating above design allowables. The nine Veldrix-9 engines mounted on the booster showed turbopump turbine erosion of 0.08 millimeters on average per blade, within the 0.15 millimeter lifetime limit allowing reuse without rework. Propellant residuals at landing were 2,400 kg of liquid oxygen and 920 kg of methane, representing 7.4 percent of the total propellant loaded, consistent with the 8 percent reserve allocation in the boostback and landing burn design. Ceridian stated that the total refurbishment cost for the first booster is 4.2 million, compared to a new booster unit cost of 38 million, validating the economic premise of the reuse program.

Interplanet Relay Corporation filed a spectrum coordination request with the Valdorian Telecommunications Regulator on March 28, 2026, for a 640-satellite V-band megaconstellation to be deployed in a shell at 1,200 km altitude in 30-degree inclination orbits, offering global broadband services at aggregate throughput of 40 Tbps. Each satellite in the proposed Nexara constellation masses 680 kg and carries a V-band phased-array antenna generating 2.8 Gbps of per-beam capacity across 12 simultaneously active spot beams. Satellite design life is 10 years, with station keeping provided by four 60 mN gridded ion thrusters burning xenon at a specific impulse of 3,200 seconds. The constellation operates in a Walker star configuration with 32 orbital planes of 20 satellites each, producing a minimum elevation angle of 40 degrees from any point between 60 degrees north and 60 degrees south latitude. Interplanet Relay estimates that the complete constellation can be deployed in 4 years using 28 dedicated rideshare launches at an average of 23 satellites per launch aboard Ondrell Targon-class vehicles. Total program cost is projected at 6.4 billion including satellite manufacturing, launch procurement, and ground segment development.

Gorven Spacecraft Systems completed full-scale structural testing of the Prism-7 lunar Gateway module on February 25, 2026, verifying structural integrity under combined internal pressure, thermal gradient, and launch vibration loads in a 19-day test campaign at the Harling Structural Test Facility. The Prism-7 is a 12.4-metric-ton habitation module with an outer diameter of 4.8 meters and a pressurized volume of 180 cubic meters, accommodating four crew members for missions of up to 30 days in cis-lunar space. The cylindrical aluminum-lithium primary structure withstood a proof pressure of 2.0 bar absolute with a safety factor of 1.5 while loaded by 18 kN of axial force simulating launch vibration coupling. Thermal cycling from minus 150 to plus 120 degrees Celsius over 8 cycles verified the integrity of the multilayer insulation blanket, the docking port seals, and the window assemblies rated for 0.1 mm/year impactor resistance. Gorven stated that the Prism-7 module is on schedule for launch in 2028 as the third element of the Drennic Lunar Gateway, following the power and propulsion element and logistics resupply module launches planned for 2027.

Alverton Aerospace Systems announced on January 12, 2026, the maiden flight of the Featherstone-2 single-engine jet trainer at the Torshaven International Aerodrome, validating the aircraft's performance envelope from stall speed through Mach 0.92 during a 78-minute sortie. The Featherstone-2 is powered by a Trevox-8 turbofan producing 28 kN of thrust, giving the aircraft a maximum speed of Mach 1.1 in a shallow dive, a service ceiling of 14,600 meters, and a ferry range of 2,600 km with a 1,200 liter external tank. Maximum takeoff weight is 7,400 kg, with a limit load factor of plus 8.0 g and minus 4.0 g at the design maneuvering speed. The glass cockpit features a three-display active matrix liquid crystal suite with a fully integrated electronic flight instrument system, a head-up display with a 30 by 22 degree field of view, and a hands-on throttle-and-stick control architecture. Alverton has launched a 28-month flight test program covering 1,200 sorties, targeting military type approval in the second quarter of 2028. Letters of intent totaling 140 aircraft have been received from six potential customers.

Solvast Research Institute published a paper on April 9, 2026, presenting numerical simulation results for a rotational detonation rocket engine operating on liquid oxygen and hydrogen at a mass flow rate of 4.2 kg/s. The simulation modeled a 12-centimeter mean diameter annular combustor with a 2-millimeter gap, capturing the propagation of three co-rotating detonation waves at a rotation frequency of 8,400 Hz. Computed specific impulse reached 438 seconds in vacuum, compared to 431 seconds for a conventional staged-combustion engine at the same mixture ratio and chamber pressure of 120 bar, representing a 1.6 percent improvement attributable to the pressure gain from the detonation process. Computed combustor wall heat flux reached 85 megawatts per square meter near the detonation wavefronts, 38 percent higher than in the equivalent deflagration combustor, requiring active cooling circuit design revisions. The paper notes that nitrogen seeding at 2 percent by mass reduced observed detonation cell size by 18 percent, which would enable a 14 percent reduction in combustor diameter for the same wave count. Solvast is in discussions with Pyraxis Systems Group about applying the technology to a 40 kN demonstrator engine by 2028.

Whitmore Navigation Solutions announced on March 5, 2026, the commercial launch of its Varis-4 precision positioning service, providing centimeter-level real-time kinematic corrections for drone and autonomous vehicle operations across the Valdorian Archipelago and the Pellaris Basin using a network of 240 reference stations. The Varis-4 service achieves horizontal positioning accuracy of 1.8 centimeters and vertical accuracy of 3.4 centimeters at a 95 percent confidence level under open-sky conditions, with performance degrading to 4.2 centimeters horizontal under 10-degree elevation angle masking. Corrections are distributed over 5G cellular links with a latency of 18 milliseconds and over L-band satellite broadcast from the Crestfall-12 geostationary satellite with a latency of 430 milliseconds for users without cellular coverage. The reference station network collects observations from all four major global navigation satellite system constellations including GPS analogue Veldris-Nav, the Drennic Compass system, and two regional constellations. Whitmore charges a subscription fee of 140 per month for light drone operators and 2,400 per month for high-density autonomous logistics operators. Over 3,800 accounts have been activated since the service soft-launched in December 2025.

Ardmore Aerospace disclosed on April 30, 2026, that it had completed preliminary design review for the Solstice-1 solar sail demonstration mission, a 16 unit CubeSat carrying a 260 square meter aluminized polyimide sail with a sail assembly mass of 3.8 kg deployed from four Bi-Stem boom arms each 8 meters long. The spacecraft, with bus dimensions of 20 by 20 by 30 centimeters and a total launch mass of 12.4 kg, is designed to demonstrate attitude control via differential reflectivity vane actuation producing torques of 2 micronewton-meters for pitch and yaw, and momentum wheel desaturation via reflectivity asymmetry for roll. Mission analysis shows that the solar radiation pressure force on the 260 square meter sail at 1 astronomical unit is 1.74 millinewtons, sufficient to achieve a heliocentric velocity increment of 0.4 km/s over a 180-day primary mission. The Solstice-1 will be launched as a secondary payload aboard an Ondrell Targon-3 rideshare mission in January 2027 to a sun-synchronous orbit at 500 km. Ardmore's team of 18 engineers has a total development budget of 9.2 million provided by the Valdorian Science Foundation.

Parvex Engineering Solutions released its annual reusable launch vehicle economic analysis on March 24, 2026, projecting that fully reusable two-stage-to-orbit vehicles in the 15,000 to 25,000 kg LEO payload class could achieve a cost to orbit of 380 to 520 per kilogram at a production and flight rate of 40 launches per year, assuming vehicle refurbishment costs of 12 percent of new vehicle unit cost and a mean flights per vehicle of 25 across the fleet. The analysis modeled propellant costs at 1.40 per kilogram of liquid methane and 0.18 per kilogram of liquid oxygen, with propellant load per mission of approximately 1,200 metric tons for a vehicle in the target payload class. Fixed infrastructure costs, amortized over a 15-year program life, contributed 88 per kilogram to the overall cost at 40 flights per year. The study noted that the economic break-even point against expendable vehicles in the same payload class occurs at approximately 22 flights per year, below which the capital cost of recovery infrastructure outweighs propellant and refurbishment savings. Parvex recommended that emerging operators targeting launch costs below 1,000 per kilogram prioritize reusability from program inception rather than as a retrofit to existing expendable designs.

The Valdorian Advanced Research Programs Office awarded a 280 million contract on April 14, 2026, to Vortran Hypersonics for development of the Meridian Boost Glide Vehicle, a maneuvering reentry vehicle designed to deliver a 600 kg payload to any point within 6,000 km range in under 30 minutes of flight time. The Meridian vehicle is boosted to approximately Mach 18 and 90 km altitude by a two-stage solid rocket propulsion system, after which it separates and glides on a depressed trajectory at altitudes between 40 and 60 km for the majority of its range. The carbon-carbon composite airframe is 4.8 meters long with a 1.2-meter span delta planform, generating a peak lift-to-drag ratio of 2.8 during mid-glide. Thermal protection relies on passive ablation of a 22 mm thick low-density carbon phenolic heat shield rated for surface temperatures up to 2,800 degrees Celsius during terminal dive. Maneuvering is achieved by bank-to-turn using four deployable aerodynamic control surfaces producing lateral accelerations of up to 4 g throughout the glide phase. Vortran indicated that wind tunnel and computational validation is complete through Mach 14 and that first flight is planned for the third quarter of 2028 from the Tressian Test Range.
